11. Schaeken, W., Van Haeren, M. & Bambini, V. (2018). Scalar Implicatures in Children With ASD: Dichotomized Responses. *Frontiers in Psychology*. PMC: PMC6064869. [Link](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6064869/)

- **Schaeken, Van Haeren & Bambini (2018)** tested 22 children with ASD and matched controls (66 total, 10-year-olds, Flemish-speaking) on scalar implicature tasks. Using a binary judgment task, ASD children performed similarly to controls. When given a three-option response (including "I agree a bit"), the results revealed that scalar inference competence is **not a yes/no matter but a fine-grained dimension** -- autistic children showed more variable tolerance for underinformative statements. PMC: PMC6064869

key: shaw|2024|barriers to healthcare and a t
bare: Shaw, S. C. K., Carravallah, L., Johnson, M., O'Sullivan, J., Chown, N., Neilson, S., & Doherty, M. (2024). "Barriers to healthcare and a 'triple empathy problem' may lead to adverse outcomes for autistic adults: A qualitative study." *Autism*, 28(7), 1746-1757. [SAGE](https://doi.org/10.1177/13623613231205629)

Shaw et al. (2024) identify a **"triple empathy problem"** in healthcare: breakdowns occur between autistic patients and clinicians, between clinicians and the healthcare system, and between the system and autistic communities.

- **Sheppard et al. (2016)** found that non-autistic participants had difficulty reading the mental states of autistic targets -- that is, the inference difficulty runs in both directions, not only from autistic to non-autistic.

key: sheppard|2016|how easy is it to read the min
bare: Sheppard, E., Pillai, D., Wong, G. T.-L., Ropar, D., & Mitchell, P. (2016). How easy is it to read the minds of people with autism spectrum disorder? *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders*, 46(4), 1247-1254. [Springer](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10803-015-2662-8)

- **Sheppard et al. (2016)** showed that non-autistic observers had difficulty inferring emotion from autistic facial displays, even though the emotion itself was accurately experienced by the target.

**Sheppard, Pillai, Wong, Ropar, and Mitchell (2016)** showed that non-autistic participants are worse at reading autistic participants' mental states than they are at reading other non-autistic participants' mental states. The "theory of mind" asymmetry runs in both directions.

bare: - **Shriberg et al. (2001)** analyzed speech and prosody in adolescents and adults with high-functioning autism and Asperger syndrome and found consistent differences in stress placement, phrasing, and resonance compared with allistic control speakers. The differences were not traceable to a single articulatory feature; they were distributed across the prosodic system. [VALIDATED]
